title: EXPOSURE XIX COHOMOLOGY OF EXCELLENT PRESCHEMAS OF EQUAL CHARACTERISTICS

pages.title: EXPOSURE XIX COHOMOLOGY OF EXCELLENT PRESCHEMAS OF EQUAL CHARACTERISTICS

blocks.html:
EXPOSURE XIX
COHOMOLOGY OF EXCELLENT PRESCHEMAS OF EQUAL CHARACTERISTICSby M. Artin
Summary

1. Purity for the ring \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_n]]\).
2. The case of a strictly local ring.
3. Purity.
4. Local acyclicity of a regular morphism.
5. Finiteness theorem.
6. Cohomological dimension of affine morphisms.
7. Affine morphisms – end of the proof.

In this presentation we develop the theory of equal cohomology for excellent pre-schemes (EGA IV 7.8.1) of equal characteristics, admitting the theorem of resolution of singularities for such pre-schemes, in the following form:

Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an excellent local scheme of equal characteristics, and let \(U \subset X\) be a regular open. There exists a proper and birational morphism \(f : X' \to X\) and an open immersion \(U \to X'\) above \(X\) such that \(X'\) is regular and \(Y = X - U\) is everywhere of codimension \(1\), with transverse intersections.
The demonstrations are therefore valid, "for the moment", only in characteristic zero [4]. We could also deduce results for the characteristic preschemes \(p > 0\) in low dimensions [1].

Under the resolution hypothesis, we will obtain more or less satisfactory results for preschemas of equal characteristics and for coefficients prime to the characteristic (cf. 3.2, 4.1, 5.1, 6.1). On the other hand, we know almost nothing about the cohomology of general preschemes of unequal characteristics, even in dimension 2, where we have the resolution [2]. For example, we do not know the purity theorem 2.1 for any complete ring of unequal characteristics of dimension \(> 1\). This result, for rings of formal series \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_n]]\) (1.2), is one of the main tools in the theory. Note also that for the proof of 1.2, we do not use the resolution theorem. This theorem is therefore demonstrated in any characteristic.

We will often use without explicit mention the properties of the excellent schemes which are developed in EGA IV 7.8, such as stability with respect to finite type extensions (EGA IV 7.8.3 (ii)) and with respect to strict localizations. This last fact results from the resolution and from EGA IV 7.9.5.
1. Purity for the ring \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_n]]\).
Recall the following corollary of XVI 3.7:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.1

Let \(A\) be a strictly local ring, and let us note \(A\{x\}\) the strict localization of \(A[x]\) in the prime ideal generated by \(\operatorname{rad} A\) and \(x\). Or \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A\{x\}[1/x]\). Then for \(n\) first to the residual characteristic of \(A\), we have \[H^q(U, \mu_n) \simeq \begin{cases} \mu_n(A) & \text{si} \quad q = 0 \\ \mathbb{Z}/n & \text{si} \quad q = 1 \\ 0 & \text{si} \quad q > 1. \end{cases}\]

Indeed, let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} A[x]\), and \(Y = V(x)\) be the locus of the zeros of \(x\) in \(X\). Then \((Y, X)\) is a smooth couple (XVI 3.1) above \(\operatorname{Spec} A\). According to (V 4, VIII 5), \(H^q(U, \mu_n)\) is identified with the fiber of the beam \(\underline{H}^{q+1}_Y(X, \mu_n)\) at a geometric point above the point \((\operatorname{rad} A, x)\) of \(X\), and the corollary therefore follows immediately from (XVI 3.7). Note that \(\mu_n\), \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) are both cyclic groups of order \(n\). We put them here to give the canonical formulas (see 3.4).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.2

Let \(k\) be a separably closed body, and \(U = \operatorname{Spec} k[[x_1, \ldots, x_r]][1/x_r]\). Then for \(n\) first to the characteristic of \(k\), we have \[H^q(U, \mu_n) = \begin{cases} \mu_n(A) & \text{si} \quad q = 0 \\ \mathbb{Z}/n & \text{si} \quad q = 1 \\ 0 & \text{si} \quad q > 1. \end{cases}\]

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note

The proof also applies to the ring of convergent series, in the case \(k = \mathbb{C}\). Furthermore, we easily avoid the assumption that \(k\) is separably closed by making the statement a little more carefully. But to establish the general purity theorem 3.2 (under the assumption of singularity resolution, of course), we need the result only in the separably closed case \(k\), and these other assertions are corollaries of this theorem 3.2.
For dimensions 0, 1, we can prove the result for each strictly local regular ring:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3

Let \(A\) be a strictly local regular ring and \(x \in \operatorname{rad} A\) be a local parameter. Or \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A[1/x]\). Then \(H^0(U, \mu_n) = \mu_n(A)\), and we have canonical isomorphisms \[\mathbb{Z}/n \;\simeq\; H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m)/(H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m))^n \;\simeq\; H^1(U, \mu_n),\] where the generator of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) is the "residue" of \(x \in H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m)\).

Demonstration. For \(q = 0\), the assertion is that \(U\) is connected and non-empty. It's true. For \(q = 1\), let us apply the exact sequence of Kummer (IX 3.7). We have \(\operatorname{Pic} U = 0\) because \(A\) is regular and factorial, and \(U\) is an open of \(X = \operatorname{Spec} A\). It follows that we have the exact sequence \[H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m) \xrightarrow{\ n\ } H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m) \longrightarrow H^1(U, \mu_n) \longrightarrow 0,\] and it remains to demonstrate that \(H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m)/(H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m))^n\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) and generated by the residue of \(x\). But we have an obvious exact sequence \[\xymatrix@C=2.5em{ 0 \ar[r] & H^0(X, \mathbb{G}_m) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \mathbb{Z} \ar[r] & 0 \\ & A^* & A[1/x]^* & & }\] where the image of a section \(a \in H^0(U, \mathbb{G}_m)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\) is the zero order of \(a\) long \(\{x = 0\}\). The group \(A^*\) is divisible by \(n\), because \(A\) is strictly local and \(n\) is prime to the residual characteristic. Indeed, if \(u \in H^0(X, \mathbb{G}_m)\), the equation \(Y^n - u = 0\) defines an equal finite extension of \(A\), which is therefore completely decomposed. It follows that \(H^1(U, \mu_n) \simeq \mathbb{Z}/n\), hence the lemma.

Demonstration of 1.2. The case \(q > 1\) remains to be processed. Let us recall the terminology of (V, appendix). We have \[H^q(U, \mu_n) = \varinjlim_{\mathbb{U}} H^q(\mathbb{U}, \mu_n)\] where \(\mathbb{U} = \left\{ U_0 \mathrel{\substack{\longleftarrow \\ \longleftarrow}} U_1 \mathrel{\substack{\longleftarrow \\ \longleftarrow \\ \longleftarrow}} \cdots \right\}\) traverses the (filtering, up to homotopy) category of hyper-overlaps of \(U\). Let \(\mathbb{U}\) be such a hypercover, and let us take separate \(U_i\) of finite type. Since we want to demonstrate that \(H^q(U, \mu_n) = 0\), it suffices to find a hyper-covering \(\mathbb{V}\) and a morphism \(\mathbb{V} \to \mathbb{U}\) such that the induced morphism \[H^q(\mathbb{U}, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(\mathbb{V}, \mu_n)\] or the null morphism.

Let \(\{W_1, \ldots, W_m\}\) be the set of connected components of all \(U_i\) for \(i \leqslant q+1\), and let \(B_j\) be the normalized ring of \(A = k[[x_1, \ldots, x_r]]\) in the field \(R(W_j)\) of rational functions on \(W_j\). Then, since \(W_j \to U\) is equal, the scheme \(W_j\) is an open of \(\operatorname{Spec} B_j\), say the complementary open to \(\operatorname{Spec} C_j\), where \(C_j\) is the reduced ring quotient of \(B_j\) suitable.

Let us also choose an element \(b_j\) of \(B_j\) such that \(b_j\) generates the separable extension \(R(W_j)\) of \(k((x_1, \ldots, x_r))\). Let \(f_j(Y) \in A[Y]\) be the irreducible unit polynomial of \(b_j\) above \(A\), so that \(B_j\) is isomorphic to the normalized of the ring \(A[Y]/(f_j)\). Finally, let \(d_j \in A\) be the discriminant of \(f_j(Y)\), which is a non-zero element of \(A\).
But it is allowed to replace the \(x_i\) for \(i < r\) by other elements of \(A\), the only condition being that \(\{x_1, \ldots, x_r\}\) must form a system of parameters of \(A\).
It is easy to see that by changing these elements if necessary, we can assume that the following hypotheses are satisfied:

1. (i) For each \(j\) such that \(d_j\) is not a unit, \(x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}, d_j\) generate a primary ideal for \(\operatorname{rad} A\).
  2. (ii) For each \(j\), the images of \(x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}\) in \(C_j\) generate a primary ideal for \(\operatorname{rad} C_j\).
Then we have the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5

Let \(A^0 = k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}]]\{x_r\}\) be the henselized of \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}]][x_r]\) at the point \((x_1, \ldots, x_r)\), let \(X^0 = \operatorname{Spec} A^0\) and \(U^0 = \operatorname{Spec} A^0[1/x_r]\). Under conditions 1.4 above, there exists a hyper-covering \(\mathbb{U}^0\) of \(U^0\) (of order \(q+1\)) and an isomorphism \(\mathbb{U}^0 \times_{X^0} X \simeq \mathbb{U}\).

Let us admit the lemma. Now the ring \(A^0\) is excellent (EGA IV 7.8.3 (ii), 7.9.5), and consequently, the connected components of \(U^0_i\) and \(U_i\) are in one-to-one correspondence (EGA IV 7.8.3 (vii)). The isomorphism \(\mathbb{U}^0 \times_{X^0} X \simeq \mathbb{U}\) therefore gives an isomorphism \[H^q(\mathbb{U}^0, \mu_n) \simeq H^q(\mathbb{U}, \mu_n).\] Since \(k\) is separably closed, \(A^0\) is a strictly local ring, and we can apply 1.1 to the ring \(A^0\). It follows that \(H^q(U^0, \mu_n) = 0\). But \[H^q(U^0, \mu_n) \simeq \varinjlim_{\mathbb{U}^0} H^q(\mathbb{U}^0, \mu_n),\] where \(\mathbb{U}^0\) traverses the category of hyperoverlaps of \(U^0\) of order \(q+1\), and \(H^q(\mathbb{U}^0, \mu_n)\) is a finite group. Consequently, there exists a hyper-covering morphism \(\mathbb{V}^0 \to \mathbb{U}^0\) such that the induced morphism \[H^q(\mathbb{U}^0, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(\mathbb{V}^0, \mu_n)\] or the null morphism. Let's set \(\mathbb{V} = \mathbb{V}^0 \times_{X^0} X\). Then \(H^q(\mathbb{V}^0, \mu_n) \simeq H^q(\mathbb{V}, \mu_n)\) (same reasoning as for \(\mathbb{U}^0\)), therefore \[H^q(\mathbb{U}, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(\mathbb{V}, \mu_n)\] is also the null morphism, cqfd.

Demonstration of 1.5. It is enough to find \(A^0\)-finite algebras \(B^0_j\), \(C^0_j\) and \(A\)-isomorphisms \[\begin{aligned} B^0_j \otimes_{A^0} A &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } B_j \\ C^0_j \otimes_{A^0} A &\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } C_j. \end{aligned}\] Indeed, according to ([3] 1.4) the morphism \(B_j \to C_j\) is induced by a morphism \(B^0_j \to C^0_j\), therefore the open \(W_j \subset \operatorname{Spec} B_j\) is induced by an open \(W^0_j \subset \operatorname{Spec} B^0_j\). Moreover, the \(U\)-morphisms between the \(W_j\) are induced by morphisms between the \(A\)-algebras \(B_j\), therefore ([3] 1.4) by morphisms between the \(B^0_j\), therefore by \(U^0\)-morphisms of \(W^0_j\). It follows that the \(W^0_j\) form the set of connected components of a simplicial object \(\mathbb{U}^0\) above \(U^0\) which induces \(\mathbb{U}\), and we immediately see that it is a hyper-covering of \(U^0\).

Or 1.4 (ii) implies that \(C_j\) is induced by a \(A^0\)-algebra \(C^0_j\), as we easily verify (∗). For \(B_j\), it suffices to find a unit polynomial \(f^0_j(Y) \in A^0[Y]\) such that the two \(A\)-algebras \[A[Y]/(f_j) \quad \text{et} \quad A[Y]/(f^0_j) = A \otimes_{A^0} A^0[Y]/(f^0_j)\] are isomorphic. Indeed, \(A^0[Y]/(f^0_j)\) is then reduced, and we take for \(B^0_j\) the normalized (EGA IV 7.8.3 (vi)) of \(A^0[Y]/(f^0_j)\). The ring \(B^0_j \otimes_{A^0} A\) is still normal (EGA IV 7.8.3 (vii)) and birational to \(B_j\), therefore equal to this ring.

According to 1.4 (i), the ideal \(I_j \subset A\) generated by \(d_j\) is induced by an ideal of \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}]][x_r]\) (prep. th. of Weierstrass [5] [6]), therefore by an ideal \(I^0_j\) of \(A^0\). Furthermore, \(A/I_j\) is a finite \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}]]\)-algebra, so is isomorphic to \(A^0/I^0_j\). This last ring is therefore complete, and consequently, the \(I^0_j\)-adic completed ring of \(A^0\) is complete and isomorphic to \(A\). We can therefore find a unit polynomial \(f^0_j \in A^0[Y]\) such that in \(A[Y]\), we have \[f_j \equiv f^0_j \quad (\text{modulo } d_j^2 \operatorname{rad} A).\] According to ([3], 1.3), \(A[Y]/(f_j) \simeq A[Y]/(f^0_j)\), hence the lemma.

footnotes.html: (∗) Indeed, 1.4 (ii) implies that \(C_j\) is finite on \(k[[x_1,\ldots,x_{r-1}]]\), and a fortiori on \(A^0\), and it suffices to take \(C^0_j = C_j\) with the structure of \(A^0\)-module obtained by restriction of scalars.

pages.title: 2. The case of a strictly local ring.

blocks.html: 2. The case of a strictly local ring.
The theorem is as follows:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1 (∗)

Let \(A\) be an excellent, regular, strictly local ring, of equal characteristics, and let \(x\) be a "local parameter" (∗∗) of \(A\). Or \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A[1/x]\). Then for \(n\) first to the characteristic of \(A\), we have \[H^q(U, \mu_n) = \begin{cases} \mu_n(A) & \text{si} \quad q = 0 \\ \mathbb{Z}/n & \text{si} \quad q = 1 \\ 0 & \text{si} \quad q > 1. \end{cases}\]
The demonstration is done by induction on \(q\). We denote by \(P(N)\) the statement of 2.1 for the values ​​of \(q \leqslant N\). According to 1.3, the assertion \(P(1)\) is true.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2

Suppose \(P(N)\) is true. Let \(A \to A'\) be a local morphism of excellent, regular, strictly local rings, of equal characteristics. Let \(\{x_1, \ldots, x_r\}\) be a part of a regular system of parameters of \(A\) of which the set of images in \(A'\) is still a part of a regular system of parameters. Let \[U = \operatorname{Spec} A[1/x_1, \ldots, 1/x_r]\] \[U' = \operatorname{Spec} A'[1/x'_1, \ldots, 1/x'_r].\] Then the canonical morphism \[H^q(U, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(U', \mu_n)\] is bijective for each \(q \leqslant N\).

Demonstration. (∗) Recursion on \(r\); the assertion is trivial for \(r = 0\). Let \(r > 0\) and suppose that the theorem is proved for \(r - 1\). Let \(B = A/(x)\), \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A[1/x_1, \ldots, 1/x_{r-1}]\), \(Y = \operatorname{Spec} B[1/x_1, \ldots, 1/x_{r-1}]\) (where we denote by the same symbol the image of \(x_i\) in \(B\)). There is an open immersion \(i : U \to V\) and \(Y\) is the complementary closed set, defined by the element \(x_r\).

By applying \(P(N)\) to the strict localized rings of \(V\) at the geometric points of \(Y\), we find that \[(2.3)\qquad R^q i_*(\mu_n)_U = \begin{cases} (\mu_n)_V & \text{si} \quad q = 0 \\ (\mathbb{Z}/n)_Y & \text{si} \quad q = 1 \\ 0 & \text{si} \quad 1 < q \leqslant N, \end{cases}\] because these strict localities are excellent regular rings of equal char., and the element \(x_r\) is a local parameter at each point of \(Y\).

Let us note with a "prime" the analogous objects deduced from \(A'\). We find a morphism of spectral sequences \[\xymatrix@C=1em@R=3em{ E_2^{pq} = H^p(V, R^q i_* \mu_n) \ar@{=>}[r] \ar[d]_{\varphi_2^{pq}} & H^{p+q}(U, \mu_n) \ar[d]^{\varphi} \\ E'^{pq}_2 = H^p(V', R^q i'_* \mu_n) \ar@{=>}[r] & H^{p+q}(U', \mu_n). }\] Now it is obvious by 1.3 and 2.3 that the change of base \(V \xleftarrow{\ g\ } V'\) induces an isomorphism \[g^* R^q i_* \mu_n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^q i'_* \mu_n\] for \(q \leqslant N\) (only for \(q = 0,1\) is there something small to check). Consequently, the recurrence hypothesis, applied to the morphism \[H^q(V, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(V', \mu_n),\] implies that \(\varphi_2^{pq}\) is an isomorphism for \(q = 0\) and \(p \leqslant N\).

Similarly, we can apply the recurrence hypothesis to the morphism of rings \(B \to B'\) — these are reg., exc., str. rings. loc., equal char., and \(x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}\) elements form part of a parameter system. So \[H^q(Y, \mu_n) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(Y', \mu_n) \quad \text{si} \quad q \leqslant N.\] Since \((\mu_n)_Y \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/n)_Y\), it follows that \(\varphi_2^{pq}\) is equally bijective for \(p \leqslant N\) and for \(q = 1\).

From the fact that \(R^q i_* \mu_n = R^q i'_* \mu_n = 0\) if \(1 < q \leqslant N\), we find \(E_2^{pq} = E'^{pq}_2 = 0\) if \(1 < q \leqslant N\), and the morphism of spectral sequences is therefore reduced for \(1 \leqslant m \leqslant N\) to a morphism of exact sequences \[\xymatrix@C=0.9em{ H^{m-2}(Y,\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] \ar[d]^{a} & H^m(V,\mu_n) \ar[r] \ar[d]^{b} & H^m(U,\mu_n) \ar[r]^-{e} \ar[d]^{c} & H^{m-1}(Y,\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] \ar[d]^{d} & H^{m+1}(V,\mu_n) \ar[d] \\ H^{m-2}(Y',\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] & H^m(V',\mu_n) \ar[r] & H^m(U',\mu_n) \ar[r] & H^{m-1}(Y',\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r] & H^{m+1}(V',\mu_n). }\] Now we have already seen that \(a\), \(b\), \(d\) are bijective, and it follows that \(c\) is injective. To demonstrate that \(c\) is surjective, it suffices to demonstrate that the morphism \[H^m(U, \mu_n) \xrightarrow{\ e\ } H^{m-1}(Y, \mathbb{Z}/n)\] is surjective for \(m \leqslant N\).

Let \(k \subset A\) be an arbitrary separably closed field, and let \(A^0 = k[x_1, \ldots, x_r]\) be the henselized of \(k[x_1, \ldots, x_r]\) at the origin. Then the assumptions of 2.1 are satisfied for the morphism \(A^0 \to A\) and for the elements \(x_i\). With the obvious notations, we have a commutative square \[\xymatrix@C=3em{ H^m(U^0,\mu_n) \ar[r]^-{e^0} \ar[d]_{c^0} & H^{m-1}(Y^0, \mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[d]^{d^0} \\ H^m(U,\mu_n) \ar[r]^-{e} & H^{m-1}(Y, \mathbb{Z}/n) }\] and we already know that the arrow \(d^0\) is bijective. It is thus sufficient to demonstrate that the morphism \(e^0\) is surjective. We are therefore brought back (by dropping the \({}^0\)) to verify the surjectivity in the case \(A = k\{x_1, \ldots, x_r\}\).

For this ring, which is the limit of smooth schemes on \(k\), we demonstrated the purity theorem in XVI 3.7. We therefore have \(R^q i_* \mu_n = 0\) for each \(q > 1\), and consequently an exact sequence \[H^m(U, \mu_n) \xrightarrow{\ e\ } H^{m-1}(Y, \mathbb{Z}/n) \longrightarrow H^{m+1}(V, \mu_n) \xrightarrow{\ f\ } H^{m+1}(U, \mu_n)\] for each \(m \geqslant 1\). The surjectivity of \(e\) is therefore equivalent to the injectivity of \(f\).

Let \(Z = \operatorname{Spec} k\{x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}\}[1/x_1, \ldots, 1/x_{r-1}]\). The morphism \(\operatorname{Spec} k\{x_1, \ldots, x_r\} \to \operatorname{Spec} k\{x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}\}\) given by the inclusion of rings is acyclic. It is the local acyclicity of a smooth morphism (XVI 1.1). Since the open \(V\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} k\{x_1, \ldots, x_r\}\) is the inverse image of \(Z\), the morphism \[H^q(Z, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(V, \mu_n)\] is bijective for each \(q\) (XV 1.3). It is therefore sufficient to demonstrate that the morphism \[H^q(Z, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(U, \mu_n)\] is injective for each \(q\).

Or \(k\{x_1, \ldots, x_r\}\) is the inductive limit of rings \(A_\alpha\) spread above \(k\{x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}\}[x_r]\), with a bearing from the point \(x_1 = x_2 = \cdots = x_r = 0\) to this ring. Consequently, \(U\) is the projective limit of the open \(U_\alpha = \operatorname{Spec} A_\alpha[1/x_1, \ldots, 1/x_r]\), and we have \[H^q(U, \mu_n) = \varinjlim_\alpha H^q(U_\alpha, \mu_n).\] But since \(A_\alpha[1/x_r]\) is smooth above the Henselian ring \(k\{x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}\}\), and the closed fiber of \(A[1/x_r]\) is non-empty, we immediately see that the \(k\{x_1, \ldots, x_{r-1}\}\)-algebra \(A_\alpha[1/x_r]\) admits sections. So \(U_\alpha\) admits sections above \(Z\), and it follows that \[H^q(Z, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(U_\alpha, \mu_n)\] is injective for each \(q\), so that \[H^q(Z, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(U, \mu_n)\] is also injective, hence the lemma.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4

Suppose \(P(N)\) is true. Let \(A \to A'\) be a regular morphism (EGA IV 6.8.1) of excellent, strictly local rings, of equal characteristics (not necessarily regular), and \(U \subset \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an open which is regular. Or \(U' = U \times_{\operatorname{Spec} A} \operatorname{Spec} A'\). So for \(n\) first to the car. res. of \(A\), the morphism \[H^q(U, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(U', \mu_n)\] is bijective for \(q \leqslant N\).

Demonstration. Let \(f : X \to \operatorname{Spec} A\) be a resolution of the singularities of \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) such that the open \(U\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) rises to an open \(i : U \to X\), and that the closed set \(Y = X - U\) is purely of codimension 1 and with transverse intersections. Let us denote by \({}'\) the change of base induced by \(A \to A'\). The morphism \(g : X' \to X\) is regular (EGA IV 6.8.3), so \(f' : X' \to \operatorname{Spec} A'\) is a resolution of \(\operatorname{Spec} A'\), \(i' : U' \to X'\) an open immersion, and \(Y' = X' - U'\) is purely of codimension 1, with transverse intersections. All these diagrams are excellent.

Let \(p'\) be a point in \(X'\) and \(p\) its image in \(X\). Since \(Y\) is cross-intersecting in \(X\), there exist elements \(x_1, \ldots, x_r \in \mathcal{O}_{X,p}\) such that \(\{x_1, \ldots, x_r\}\) is part of a parameter system of \(\mathcal{O}_{X,p}\) and \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X,p} \cap Y = V(x_1, \ldots, x_r)\). Moreover, it immediately follows from the fact that \(g : X' \to X\) is regular that the images \(x'_i\) of \(x_i\) in \(\mathcal{O}_{X',p'}\) form part of a system of parameters of this ring, and that we have \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{X',p'} \cap Y = V(x'_1, \ldots, x'_r)\).

Let \(B'\) be the strict localization of \(\mathcal{O}_{X',p'}\) at a geometric point \(\bar{p}\) above \(p'\), and let \(B\) be the strict localization of \(\mathcal{O}_{X,p}\) at the geometric point of \(X\) corresponding (these are rings excellent (EGA IV 7.8.3 (ii), 7.9.5)). Let \[U_{\bar{p}} = \operatorname{Spec} B[1/x_1, \ldots, 1/x_r], \qquad U'_{\bar{p}} = \operatorname{Spec} B'[1/x'_1, \ldots, 1/x'_r].\] Lemma 2.2 is applicable, and we deduce that the morphisms \[(*)\qquad H^q(U_{\bar{p}}, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^q(U'_{\bar{p}}, \mu_n)\] are bijective for \(q \leqslant N\).

Let us calculate the beams \(R^q i_* \mu_n\), \(R^q i'_* \mu_n\) for the open immersions \(i : U \to X\) and \(i' : U' \to X'\): the fiber \((R^q i'_* \mu_n)_{\bar{p}}\) at the geometric point \(\bar{p}\) is none other than \(H^q(U'_{\bar{p}}, \mu_n)\) (VIII 5). Likewise, \((R^q i_* \mu_n)_{\bar{p}} = H^q(U_{\bar{p}}, \mu_n)\). So \((*)\) implies that base change morphisms \[(**)\qquad g^* R^q i_* \mu_n \longrightarrow R^q i'_* \mu_n\] are bijective for \(q \leqslant N\).

According to the base change theorem for a proper morphism (XII 5.5), we have (since \(X\) and \(X'\) are proper above strictly local ring spectra, and the closed fibers of \(X\) and \(X'\) are equal) \[H^q(X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(X', g^* F)\] whatever the torsion beam \(F\), for each \(q\). Consequently, \((**)\) implies that in the morphism of spectral sequences \[\xymatrix@C=1em@R=3em{ E_2^{pq} = H^p(X, R^q i_* \mu_n) \ar@{=>}[r] \ar[d]_{\varphi_2^{pq}} & H^{p+q}(U, \mu_n) \ar[d]^{\varphi} \\ E'^{pq}_2 = H^p(X', R^q i'_* \mu_n) \ar@{=>}[r] & H^{p+q}(U', \mu_n), }\] the arrows \(\varphi_2^{pq}\) are bijective for each \(q \leqslant N\). It is easy to see that this implies that \(\varphi_\infty^{pq}\) is bijective if \(q \leqslant N\) and \(p+q \leqslant N+1\). Consequently, the morphism of the outcomes \[H^m(U, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^m(U', \mu_n)\] is bijective for \(m \leqslant N\), hence the lemma.
The following lemma will complete the proof of 2.1:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.5

\(P(N) \Longrightarrow P(N+1)\).

Demonstration. Let \(A\) be strictly local, regular, excellent, of equal characteristics, \(x \in \operatorname{rad} A\) a local parameter, \(U = \operatorname{Spec} A[1/x]\). Let \(\hat{A}\) be the complement of \(A\), \(\hat{x}\) the image element of \(x\), \(\hat{U} = \operatorname{Spec} \hat{A}[1/x]\), and \(g : \hat{U} \to U\) the canonical morphism. It is enough to demonstrate that we have \[(2.6)\qquad \begin{aligned} g_*(\mu_n)_{\hat{U}} &\simeq (\mu_n)_U \quad \text{et} \\ R^q g_* \mu_n &= 0 \quad \text{si} \quad 1 \leqslant q \leqslant N. \end{aligned}\] Indeed, Leray's spectral sequence \[E_2^{pq} = H^p(U, R^q g_* \mu_n) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\hat{U}, \mu_n)\] will give isomorphisms \[H^q(U, \mu_n) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(\hat{U}, \mu_n)\] for \(q \leqslant N\), and an exact sequence \[0 \longrightarrow H^{N+1}(U, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^{N+1}(\hat{U}, \mu_n) \longrightarrow H^0(U, R^{N+1} g_* \mu_n).\] But we already know that \(P(1)\) is true (1.3), and it is therefore a matter of proving that \(H^q(\hat{U}, \mu_n) = 0\) if \(1 < q \leqslant N+1\). We can thus replace \(A\) by \(\hat{A}\), which is isomorphic to a ring of formal series \(\hat{A} \simeq k[[x_1, \ldots, x_r]]\) with \(k\) separably closed and \(\hat{x} = x_r\). We dealt with this case in 1.2.

Let us therefore verify (2.6): the fact that \(g_*(\mu_n)_{\hat{U}} \simeq (\mu_n)_U\) is only the assertion of (2.3) for \(q = 0\). Recall that \(R^q g_* \mu_n\) is the beam associated with the pre-beam \(\mathcal{Q}^q\), where \[\mathcal{Q}^q(V) = H^q(\hat{V}, \mu_n)\] for \(V \to U\) spread. It is enough to take \(V\) separated and of finite type, then \(V\) is the sum of integrity schemes, and we only need to look at such \(V\). Let \(B\) be the normalized of \(A\) in the field of fractions of \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_V)\). Then \(B\) is a finite \(A\)-algebra (EGA IV 7.8.3 (vi)) and we immediately see that \(V\) is a regular open set of \(\operatorname{Spec} B\) and that \(\hat{V}\) is the corresponding open set of \(\operatorname{Spec} \hat{B}\), where \(\hat{B} = B \otimes_A \hat{A}\). It follows that the assumptions of 2.4 are satisfied for the open \(V \subset \operatorname{Spec} B\), and for the morphism \(B \to \hat{B}\). We therefore have \[\mathcal{Q}^q(V) \simeq H^q(V, \mu_n) \simeq H^q(\hat{V}, \mu_n)\] for \(q \leqslant N\). But the beam associated with the pre-beam \(V \mapsto H^q(V, \mu_n)\) is obviously zero, hence the lemma.

footnotes.html: (∗) Depends on the resolution of singularities (see Introduction).
(∗∗) What is meant here: element forming part of a regular system of parameters.

(∗) A shorter proof than the one that follows, but using the cup-product formalism, would consist of showing that under the hypothesis \(P(N)\), the cohomology \(H^*(U,\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})\) is, in dimension \(\leqslant N\), the exterior algebra (on \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)) of \(H^1(U,\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}) \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r\), which leads to establishing 2.2 for \(q = 1\), well-known case. The calculation indicated under the hypothesis \(P(N)\) is explained for example in SGA 7.

blocks.html: 3. Purity.
We will adopt terminology similar to that of XVI 3:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

We call a regular pair \((Y, X)\) of codimension \(c\) a closed immersion \(i : Y \to X\) of locally regular Noetherian schemes such that for each \(y \in Y\) we have \(\operatorname{codim}_y(Y, X) = c\) (then there exists an open neighborhood \(X'\) of \(y\) in \(X\) and sections \(x_1, \ldots, x_c \in \Gamma(X', \mathcal{O}_X)\) which generate the ideal of \(Y\), and which form part of a regular system of parameters of \(X\) at each point of \(Y \cap X'\)). We note \(U = X - Y\), and \(j : U \to X\) the corresponding open immersion.

A morphism \((Y', X') \to (Y, X)\) of regular pairs of codimension \(c\) is a morphism \(X' \to X\) such that \(Y' = Y \times_X X'\). We define in an obvious analogous way the notion of regular triple, regular triple morphism, etc.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2 (∗)

(Cohomological purity) Let \((Y, X)\) be a regular couple of codimension \(c > 0\), where \(X\) is an excellent preschema of equal characteristics. Let \(F\) be a locally constant sheaf on \(X\) of cyclic groups of order \(n\) prime with the characteristic of \(X\). So we have, in the notation of (V 4, VIII 5) \[\underline{H}^q_Y(X, F) = (R^q i^!)F = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q \neq 2c,\] \[(R^q j_*)j^* F = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q \neq 0, 2c-1, \quad \text{et}\] \[\underline{H}^{2c}_Y(X, F) = i^*(R^{2c-1} j_*)j^* F\] is a locally constant sheaf of cyclic groups of order \(n\) on \(Y\).

Demonstration. (cf. XVI 3.7). We immediately see that we have \[\underline{H}^q_Y(X, F) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q = 0,1.\] According to (V 4.5) \[\underline{H}^q_Y(X, F) = (R^q i^!)F \simeq i^*(R^{q-1} j_*)j^* F \quad \text{si} \quad q > 1,\] therefore the assertions for the three bundles are equivalent. Since the assertions are local to \(X\) for the sparse topology, we can assume that \(F = \mu_n\).

For the case \(c = 1\), the calculation of the fibers (VIII 5) of \((R^q j_*)j^* \mu_n\) brings us back to the situation envisaged in 2.1, hence \[((R^q j_*)j^* \mu_n)_{\bar{y}} = \begin{cases} \mathbb{Z}/n & \text{si} \quad q = 1 \\ 0 & \text{si} \quad q > 1 \end{cases}\] for each geometric point \(\bar{y}\) of \(Y\). Since these sheaves are zero outside of \(Y\) in any case, this proves the assertion for \(q > 1\). For \(q = 1\) (\(= 2c-1\)), we apply the criterion of (IX 2.13 (i)) and the explicit calculation of 1.3.

The case \(c > 1\) is treated by induction (see XVI 3.7): Let \((Y, X)\) be a regular couple of codimension \(c > 1\). From the definition, it is clear that, locally on \(X\), we can find a regular triple \((Y, Z, X)\) where \((Z, X)\) is of codimension 1 and \((Y, Z)\) is of codimension \(c-1\). Let \[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.2em{ Y \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]_{i} & & Z \ar[dl]^{v} \\ & X & }\] closed immersions. We have the spectral sequence \[E_2^{pq} = (R^p u^!)(R^q v^!)F \Longrightarrow (R^{p+q} i^!)F.\] By induction hypothesis, \((R^q v^!)F = 0\) if \(q \neq 2\), and is locally constant etc ... if \(q = 2\). So, again by induction hypothesis, applied to the morphism \(u\), \((R^p u^!)(R^q v^!)F = 0\) if \((p,q) \neq (2c-2, 2)\), and is loc. const. etc ... if \((p,q) = (2c-2, 2)\), hence the result for the outcome \((R^{p+q} i^!)F\), oqfd.

3.3. It now remains to determine the locally constant sheaves of (3.2) up to canonical isomorphism. We introduce for each prescheme \(S\) the locally constant sheaves \[(\mu_n^{\otimes r})_S = \text{le produit tensoriel } r\text{-ième du faisceau } (\mu_n)_S\] above the constant beam of rings \(\mathbb{Z}_S\). Let's also set \((\mu_n^{\otimes 0})_S = (\mathbb{Z}/n)_S\). We immediately see that if \(n\) is invertible on \(S\), then \((\mu_n^{\otimes r})_S\) is a locally constant sheaf of cyclic groups of order \(n\). We have formulas of the type \[(\mu_n^{\otimes r}) \otimes (\mu_n^{\otimes s}) = (\mu_n^{\otimes r+s}),\] and if \(S' \to S\), then \((\mu_n^{\otimes r})_{S'}\) is canonically isomorphic to the inverse image on \(S'\) of \((\mu_n^{\otimes r})_S\). We will often omit the symbol \(S\) if there is no concern for confusion.

Let \((Y, X)\) be a regular couple of codimension 1, and suppose that \(Y\) is defined by an equation \(x = 0\). We have the exact sequence of beams \[0 \longrightarrow (\mathbb{G}_m)_X \longrightarrow j_*(\mathbb{G}_m)_U \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}_Y \longrightarrow 0,\] hence by applying (1.3), canonical isomorphisms \[(3.3.1)\qquad (R^1 j_* \mu_n) \simeq j_*(\mathbb{G}_m)_U/(j_*(\mathbb{G}_m)_U)^n \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/n)_Y,\] which obviously do not depend on the choice of \(x\). Consequently, we have a canonical isomorphism \[(3.3.2)\qquad \varphi : \mathbb{Z}/n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (R^2 i^!)\mu_n \simeq i^*(R^1 j_*)\mu_n.\]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.4 (∗)

Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be the family of regular pairs of excellent patterns, with equal characteristics. There exists one function and only one \(\varphi\) giving for each regular pair \((Y, X)\) of codimension \(c\) an isomorphism (called canonical) of sheaves \[\varphi(Y, X) = \varphi : \mathbb{Z}/n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{H}^{2c}_Y(X, \mu_n^{\otimes c}) = (R^{2c} i^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c}\] such that \(\varphi\) satisfies the following conditions:

(i) If \(c = 1\), and if \(Y\) is defined in \(X\) by an equation \(x = 0\), then \(\varphi\) is the morphism (3.3).

(ii) \(\varphi\) is compatible with smooth pair morphisms, that is to say, if \((Y', X') \to (Y, X)\) is a morphism, let \(g : Y' \to Y\) be the induced morphism. The \(Y'\)-beam diagram \[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ \mathbb{Z}/n \ar[r]^-{\varphi(Y',X')} \ar[d] & (R^{2c} i^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c} \ar[d] \\ g^*(\mathbb{Z}/n) \ar[r]^-{g^* \varphi(Y,X)} & g^*(R^{2c} i^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c} }\] is commutative (which implies in particular that the right vertical arrow is an isomorphism).

(iii) Transitivity: let \((Z, Y, X)\) be a regular triple, that is to say, a commutative diagram of closed immersion of regular preschemas etc..., \[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.2em{ Z \ar[rr]^{v} \ar[dr]_{w} & & Y \ar[dl]^{u} \\ & X & }\] Let \(a, b, c\) be the codimensions of \(u, v, w\) respectively (therefore \(c = a+b\)). We have an isomorphism \[\varphi(Y, X) \otimes \mu_n^{\otimes b} : \mu_n^{\otimes b} \longrightarrow (R^{2a} u^!)\mu_n^{\otimes a} \otimes \mu_n^{\otimes b} \simeq (R^{2a} u^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c}.\] The spectral sequence \((R^p v^!)(R^q u^!)F \Longrightarrow (R^{p+q} w^!)F\) gives an isomorphism \[\xi : (R^{2b} v^!)(R^{2a} u^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (R^{2c} w^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c}.\] We thus obtain an isomorphism diagram \[(3.4.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=4em@R=3.2em{ \mathbb{Z}/n \ar[r]^-{\varphi(Z,Y)} \ar[d]_{\varphi(Z,X)} & (R^{2b} v^!)\mu_n^{\otimes b} \ar[d]^{\eta} \\ (R^{2c} w^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c} & (R^{2b} v^!)(R^{2a} u^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c} \ar[l]^-{\xi} }\] where \(\eta = (R^{2b} v^!)(\varphi(Y,X) \otimes \mu_n^{\otimes b})\), and the transitivity assertion is that this diagram is commutative.

Demonstration. The demonstration of existence and uniqueness is done by induction on the codimension \(c\). For \(c = 1\), we define the morphism \(\varphi\) locally with (3.3.1). Since this morphism does not depend on the choice of the parameter \(x\), we obtain an isomorphism \(\varphi\) for each \((Y, X)\) of codimension 1 by joining, and it is clear, according to the definition of the morphism and of 1.3, that the \(\varphi\) thus constructed satisfies (ii). Transitivity does not come into play for \(c = 1\).

Now suppose that existence and uniqueness are already proven if the codimension is \(< c\), and \(c > 1\). By applying the recurrence hypothesis, we immediately see that in the situation of 3.4 (iii) the three arrows of 3.4.1 which are already defined, i.e. \(\varphi(Z,Y)\), \(\xi\), \(\eta\) are compatible with the morphisms of regular triples. Let's pose in this situation \[\Psi(Z,Y,X) = \xi\, \eta\, \varphi(Z,Y),\] so \(\Psi\) is also compatible with triple morphisms. Now if \((Z,X)\) is a regular couple of codimension \(c > 1\), we can, locally on \(X\), find a triple \((Z,Y,X)\) with \(0 < \operatorname{codim}(Y,X) < c\). Everything therefore amounts to demonstrating that the arrow \(\Psi(Z,Y,X)\) is independent of \(Y\). Indeed, this will demonstrate the uniqueness of \(\varphi\) for the codimension \(c\), and the existence will result by joining.

If \((Z,Y,X)\) is arbitrary such that \(\operatorname{codim}(Y,X) > 1\), we can, locally on \(X\), find a regular triple \((Y,W,X)\) where the codimension of \((W,X)\) is equal to 1. By hunting the transitivity diagram, which we leave to the reader, we therefore come back to verifying the independence of \(Y\) when \((Y,X)\) is of codimension 1.

Let \((Z,Y_0,X)\) and \((Z,Y_1,X)\) be two such triples. It is enough to do the verification fiber by fiber, and we are therefore brought back to the strictly local case \(X\), and \(Y_i\) defined by an equation, say \(f_i = 0\) (\(i=0,1\)). Let \(\bar{X} = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_X[t]\), \(\bar{Z} = Z \times_X \bar{X}\), and \(\bar{Y}\) be the closed subschema of \(\bar{X}\) defined by the equation \[(f_1 - f_0)t + f_0 = 0.\] Then \(\bar{Y}\) is obviously regular at the points above the sections \(\varepsilon_0 : \{t=0\}\) and \(\varepsilon_1 : \{t=1\}\) of \(\bar{X}/X\). Let \(C\) be the closed subset (EGA IV 7.8.3 (iv)) of \(\bar{Y}\) of the points where \(\bar{Y}\) is not regular, and replace \(\bar{X}, \bar{Y}, \bar{Z}\) by \(\bar{X} - C\), \(\bar{Y} - C\), \(\bar{Z} - C \cap \bar{Z}\) respectively. Then \((\bar{Z}, \bar{Y}, \bar{X})\) is a regular triple, and the sections \(\varepsilon_i\) give morphisms of triples \[(Z, Y_i, X) \longrightarrow (\bar{Z}, \bar{Y}, \bar{X}) \quad (i=0,1).\]

Let \(F = (R^{2c} w^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c}\) be the beam on \(Z\). It is a locally constant beam, isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) (by one or other of the isomorphisms \(\Psi(Z, Y_i, X)\)), and we immediately see that the beam \(\bar{F} = (R^{2c} \bar{w}^!)\mu_n^{\otimes c}\) on \(\bar{Z}\) is the inverse image on \(\bar{Z}\) of \(F\). Since the fibers of \(\bar{Z} \to Z\) are connected non-empty and \(\bar{Z} \to Z\) is smooth, therefore open, it follows that the morphism deduces \[H^0(Z, F) \longrightarrow H^0(\bar{Z}, \bar{F})\] is bijective, therefore each of the morphisms \[(*)\qquad \xymatrix@R=1.4em@C=3em{ & & H^0(Z, F) \\ H^0(Z, F) \ar[r] & H^0(\bar{Z}, \bar{F}) \ar[ur]^{\varepsilon_0^*} \ar[dr]_{\varepsilon_1^*} & \\ & & H^0(Z, F) }\] is bijective, so that \(\varepsilon_0^* = \varepsilon_1^*\).

But giving an isomorphism of a beam \(F\) with \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) is equivalent to giving a global section (image of 1) which is a generator. Let \(\bar{\alpha}\), \(a_i\) be the global sections of \(\bar{F}\), \(F\) given by the isomorphisms \(\Psi(\bar{Z}, \bar{Y}, \bar{X})\), \(\Psi(Z, Y_i, X)\) respectively. Since \(\Psi(.,.,.)\) is compatible with morphisms, the diagram \((*)\) implies that \[a_0 = \varepsilon_0^*(\bar{\alpha}) = \varepsilon_1^*(\bar{\alpha}) = a_1,\] hence \(\Psi(Z, Y_0, X) = \Psi(Z, Y_1, X)\), cqfd.

footnotes.html: (∗) Depends on the resolution of singularities (see Introduction).

(∗) Depends on the resolution of singularities (see Introduction). This theorem can also be considered as the conjunction of 3.2 and the variance theory of Exp XVIII by the "local fundamental classes", where we study homomorphisms \(\varphi(Y,X)\) (not necessarily bijective) for \(Y\) locally complete intersection in \(X\).

pages.title: 4. Local acyclicity of a regular morphism.

blocks.html: 4. Local acyclicity of a regular morphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1 (∗)

(local acyclicity). Let \(g : X' \to X\) be a regular morphism (EGA IV 6.8.1) of excellent schemes of equal characteristics. Then \(g\) is universally locally acyclic for \(n\) prime to the characteristic.

Demonstration. The hypothesis being stable by change of base of finite type, it immediately follows from XV 1.13 ii) that it suffices to prove that \(g\) is locally acyclic for \(n\). Let us apply the criterion of XV 1.17: Let us \(\bar{x}'\) a geometric point of \(X'\) And \(\bar{x}\) the geometric point of \(X\) corresponding. It immediately follows from the definition of regular morphism that the morphism of strict localized at these points induced by \(g\) is still regular. We are therefore brought back in case \(X = \operatorname{Spec} A\) And \(X' = \operatorname{Spec} A'\) are strictly local, \(g\) and a local morphism, and according to XV 1.17 it suffices to demonstrate that the geometric fibers of \(g\) are acyclic for \(n\). By replacing \(X\) by an integrated closed subschema and \(X'\) by the corresponding closed, we return to the case of the generic geometric fiber \(X'_{\bar{x}}\). Let's take \(\bar{x} = \operatorname{Spec} \bar{K}\) Or \(\bar{K}\) is the separable closure of the field of rational functions on \(X\). So we have \[\bar{x} = \varprojlim_\alpha U_\alpha\] Or \(U_\alpha\) browses a filter category of \(X\)-related flat patterns, which can be assumed to be regular (EGA IV 7.8.3 (v)). Either \(B_\alpha\) the normalized of \(A\) in the body of rational functions \(R(U_\alpha)\). Let's ask \(U'_\alpha = X' \times_X U_\alpha\) And \(B'_\alpha = A' \otimes_A B_\alpha\). SO \(U_\alpha\) is a regular open of \(\operatorname{Spec} B_\alpha\) And \(B_\alpha \to B'_\alpha\) is regular, therefore by 2.4 \[H^q(U_\alpha, \mu_n) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(U'_\alpha, \mu_n)\] for each \(q\). Gold fiber \(X'_{\bar{x}}\) is limit of \(U'_\alpha\), SO \[\begin{aligned} H^q(X'_{\bar{x}}, \mu_n) &= \varinjlim_\alpha H^q(U'_\alpha, \mu_n) \\ &= \varinjlim_\alpha H^q(U_\alpha, \mu_n) \\ &= H^q(\bar{x}, \mu_n) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > 0, \quad \text{cqfd.} \end{aligned}\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.2

(change of base by a regular morphism). Either \[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=3em{ X \ar[d]_{f} & X' \ar[l]_{g} \ar[d]^{f'} \\ Y & Y' \ar[l]^{g} }\] a Cartesian diagram, \(g : Y' \to Y\) being a regular morphism of excellent pre-diagrams of equal characteristics, \(f\) quasi-compact and quasi-separate. Then for each sheaf \(F\) abelian of prime torsion with the characteristic, the base change morphisms (XII 4.2) \[g^*(R^q f_*)F \longrightarrow (R^q f'_*)g'^* F\] are bijective for each \(q \geqslant 0\).
This results from 4.1 and XVI 1.1.
From 4.1 and XV 1.17 we deduce immediately

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

Let \(A\) be a strictly local excellent ring of equal characteristics, and \(f : X \to \operatorname{Spec} A\) be a morphism. Let \(A \to A'\) be a regular morphism, where \(A'\) is also strictly local, excellent, of equal characteristics. Let us denote by \({}'\) the change of base induced by \(A \to A'\). For each torsion beam \(F\) on \(X\), first with the characteristic of \(A\), we have \[H^q(X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(X', F')\] for each \(q \geqslant 0\).

This corollary applies in particular in the case where \(A'\) is the complement \(\hat{A}\) of \(A\). (In this case, we can also replace the hypothesis "\(A\) strictly local" by "\(A\) local Henselian", as we immediately see.)

footnotes.html: (∗) Depends on the resolution of singularities (see Introduction).

blocks.html: 5. Finitude theorem.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1 (∗)

(finitude). Let \(f : X \to S\) be a finite type morphism of excellent schemes of equal characteristics, and let \(F\) be an abelian beam of prime torsion with the characteristic on \(X\), which is a constructible sheaf. Then the \((R^q f_*)F\) are also constructible for each \(q\).
This result generalizes the theorem of XVI 5.1. The demonstration is approximately the same: we apply the purity result 3.2 instead of XVI 3.7.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2

Let \(A\) be a strictly local excellent ring of equal characteristics and \(f : X \to \operatorname{Spec} A\) be a finite type morphism. Let \(F\) be an abelian beam on \(X\) of prime torsion with the characteristic of \(A\), and constructible. Then the \(H^q(X, F)\) are finite groups for each \(q\).

footnotes.html: (∗) Depends on the resolution of singularities (see Introduction).

blocks.html: 6. Cohomological dimension of affine morphisms.

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a finite type morphism of excellent preschemas, and \(F\) be a sheaf on \(X\). Recall that we defined (XIV 2.2) an integer \(\delta(F)\) in the case where \(Y\) is local. We can therefore look at \(\delta\) as a function \(\delta(F,f)\) on an arbitrary \(Y\), by setting \[(6.0)\qquad \delta(F,f,y) = \delta(F'),\] or \(Y' = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_{Y,y}\), \(X' \to Y'\) is the morphism deduced from \(f\) by the change of base \(Y' \to Y\), and \(F'\) is the inverse image beam of \(F\) on \(X'\).
We have the following result, which generalizes XIV 3.1:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.1 (∗)

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an affine morphism of finite type, where \(Y\) is an excellent scheme of equal characteristics, and let \(F\) be an abelian torsion sheaf on \(X\). Then \(R^q f_* F\) is zero at each point \(y \in Y\) such that \(\delta(F,f,y) < q\).
The local variant, obviously equivalent to 6.1, is as follows:

blocks.label: 6.1 bis
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.1 bis (∗)

Let \(A\) be a strictly local excellent ring of equal characteristics, and \(f : X \to Y = \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an affine morphism of finite type. Let \(F\) be a torsion beam on \(X\). So \[H^q(X, F) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > \delta(F).\]
Recall (X 5.1) that there is no interest in such assertions for the \(F\) of \(p\)-torsion, where \(p\) is equal to the characteristic. We can therefore assume \(F\) first for the characteristic.
We have the following corollary:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2

Let \(A\) be an excellent Henselian ring, with equal characteristics, with residual body \(k\), and let \(U \subset \operatorname{Spec} A\) be an open affine. Or \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\). We have \[\operatorname{cd}_\ell U \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell k + \dim A.\]

Indeed, let \(\tilde{A}\) be the strict localized of \(A\), which is an infinite galoisian covering, of group \(G = G(\bar{k}/k)\), and let \(\tilde{U}\) be the open affine of \(\operatorname{Spec} \tilde{A}\) induced by \(U\). By applying the Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence (VIII 8.4) \[H^p(G, H^q(\tilde{U}, .)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(U, .)\] we come back to treating the case \(A = \tilde{A}\), and then it is a special case of 6.1 bis.

With the notations above, let \(R(A)\) be the ring of rational functions on \(A\). We have \(\operatorname{Spec} R(A) = \varprojlim_\alpha U_\alpha\) where \(U_\alpha\) is an open affine of \(\operatorname{Spec} A\). It therefore results from the theory of passage to the limit that we have

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3

Let \(A\) excellent Henselian of equal characteristics, with residual body \(k\). Let \(R(A)\) be the body of rational functions on \(\operatorname{Spec} A\). So for \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\), we have \[\operatorname{cd}_\ell R(A) \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell k + \dim A\]
with equality if \(\ell\) is invertible in \(k\), and if in addition \(\ell \neq 2\) or \(k\) is not orderable.
Equality in the last case results from X 2.4.
Finally, by applying X 4.2, 4.4, we find

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.4

Let \(X\) be an excellent Noetherian scheme of equal characteristics, and \(R(X)\) be the ring of rational functions on \(X\). Let \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\) be invertible on \(X\), and suppose that \(\ell \neq 2\) or that no residual body of \(X\) is orderable. So \[\operatorname{cd}_\ell(X) \leqslant \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X) + \dim X \leqslant 2 \operatorname{cd}_\ell R(X).\]

Demonstration of 6.1. We begin with a reduction analogous to that of XIV 3. Recurrence on \(\delta\) : It is clear that 6.1 for \(\delta \leqslant d\) is equivalent to (6.1 bis) for \(\delta \leqslant d\). For \(\delta = 0\), it's trivial. Let's handle the case \(\delta \leqslant 1\), in the form 6.1 bis. Since \(F\) is the limit of its constructible sub-sheaves (IX 2.9 (iii)), we can assume \(F\) constructible, and then the support of \(F\) is contained in a closed \(Z \subset X\) with \(\delta(z) \leqslant 1\) for each \(z \in Z\) (XIV 2.2). Each irreducible component reduced \(Z_i\) of \(Z\) is then, or else an affine diagram of finite dimension type \(\leqslant 1\) above closed point \(y\) of \(Y\), or else a quasi-finite scheme above a reduced closed sub-schema \(C\) of \(Y\) of dimension 1. In the latter case, we have \(C = \operatorname{Spec} B\) Or \(B\) is a strictly local integral ring of dimension 1. Such a scheme \(Z_i\) is the spectrum of a strictly local integrated ring, of dimension \(\leqslant 1\), or a finite field above the field of fractions of \(B\). So the cohomological dimension of \(Z_i\) is at most equal to 1 in any case, according to X 2.3 and IX 5.7. We have an exact sequence \[0 \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow \prod_i F_i \longrightarrow \varepsilon \longrightarrow 0\] Or \(F_i\) is the restriction of \(F\) has \(Z_i\), from which we get the result for \(Z\).
Now suppose that the theorem is proved for values ​​of \(\delta < d\), and prove it for \(\delta(F) \leqslant d\), assuming \(d \geqslant 2\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.5

It is enough to treat the case where \(X = \mathbb{E}^1_Y = \operatorname{Spec} \mathcal{O}_Y[t]\) is the affine space of dimension 1, and where \(Y\) is strictly local.

The proof is that of XIV 4.2: It is clear that we can assume \(Y\) strictly local, and that \(\delta(F) \leqslant d\). It is necessary to prove that \(H^q(X,F) = 0\) if \(q > d\). We can dive \(X\) into \(\mathbb{E}^N_Y\), so we only need to deal with the case \(X = \mathbb{E}^N_Y\). Recursion on \(N\): Consider the diagram \[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.2em{ \mathbb{E}^N_Y \ar[rr]^{g} \ar[dr]_{f} & & \mathbb{E}^{N-1}_Y \ar[dl]^{h} \\ & Y & }\] so that \(\mathbb{E}^N_Y\) is the 1-dimensional affine space on \(\mathbb{E}^{N-1}_Y\). By applying the recurrence hypothesis and Leray's spectral sequence \[H^p(\mathbb{E}^{N-1}_Y, R^q g_* F) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\mathbb{E}^N_Y, F)\] we see that it is enough to demonstrate that \[\delta(R^q g_* F) \leqslant d-q.\] This means that if \(z \in \mathbb{E}^{N-1}_Y\) is a point such that \(\delta(z) > d-q\), then \(R^q g_* F = 0\) at the point \(z\). But the induction hypothesis on \(N\) applies to the morphism \(g\), and we therefore have \(R^q g_* F = 0\) at the point \(z\) if \(\delta(F,g,z) < q\). It is thus sufficient to demonstrate the inequality \[\delta(F,g,z) + \delta(z) \leqslant \delta(F).\] We leave this pleasure to the reader.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.6

To prove 6.1 for the values of \(\delta \leqslant d\), it suffices to prove this: Let \(B\) be a complete normal local ring of equal characteristics and dimension \(d\). Let \(U \subset \operatorname{Spec} B\) be an open affine. Suppose there exists an element \(x \in \operatorname{rad} B\) which is invertible on \(U\). So \[H^q(U, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d.\]
This lemma is also a special case of corollary 6.2.

Demonstration. (cf. XIV 4.4). According to 6.5, it is enough to treat the case \(Y\) strictly local and \(X = \mathbb{E}^1_Y\). Consider the diagram \[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.2em{ \mathbb{E}^1_Y \ar[rr]^{i} \ar[dr]_{f} & & \mathbb{P}^1_Y \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & Y & }\] where \(\mathbb{P}^1_Y\) is the projective space and \(\mathbb{E}^1_Y\) is deduced from \(\mathbb{P}^1\) by removing the section \(Y_\infty\) at infinity. Let \(F\) be a torsion beam on \(\mathbb{E}^1\), with \(\delta(F) \leqslant d\). We have the spectral sequence \[H^p(\mathbb{P}^1, R^q i_* F) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\mathbb{E}^1, F).\] But the \(R^q i_* F\) for \(q > 0\) are concentrated on the section \(Y_\infty\) which is isomorphic to \(Y\). Since \(Y\) is strictly local, it follows that \(H^p(\mathbb{P}^1, R^q i_* F) = 0\) if \(p > 0\) and \(q > 0\). Furthermore, according to XII 5.3, we have \(H^p(\mathbb{P}^1, i_* F) = 0\) if \(p > 2\). As we want to demonstrate that \(H^n(\mathbb{E}^1, F) = 0\) if \(n > d\), and that \(d \geqslant 2\), it suffices to demonstrate that \[H^0(\mathbb{P}^1, R^q i_* F) = H^0(Y_\infty, R^q i_* F) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d.\] This group is isomorphic to the fiber from \(R^q i_* F\) at the point \(y_\infty\) to infinity of \(\mathbb{P}^1\) in the closed fiber.

But it is enough (VII 3.3 and IX 2.9 (iii)) to treat the constructible case \(F\). Then since \(\delta(F) \leqslant d\), there exists a closed subschema \(X\) of \(\mathbb{P}^1\) with \(\dim X \leqslant d\) such that \(X\) contains support for \(F\). Let \(\operatorname{Spec} A = \tilde{X}\) be the strict localization of \(X\) at the point \(y_\infty\), \(\tilde{U} = \tilde{X} - \tilde{X} \times_{\mathbb{P}^1} Y_\infty\), and \(\tilde{F}\) be the induced beam of \(F\) on \(\tilde{U}\). Note that \(U\) is deduced from \(\tilde{X}\) by locating with respect to an element \(x \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). We have \[(R^q i_* F)_{y_\infty} \simeq H^q(\tilde{U}, \tilde{F}).\] It is thus sufficient to demonstrate that \(H^q(\tilde{U}, F) = 0\), if \(q > d\), for each torsion beam \(\tilde{F}\) on \(\tilde{U}\).

We can now apply IX 5.6, with \(\varphi = \delta\), and we find that it is enough to demonstrate that \[H^q(V, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > \delta(V)\] for each \(V\) finite and integrated above \(\tilde{U}\) and for \(\ell\) invertible. Let \(\pi : \bar{V} \to V\) be the normalized of \(V\). We have the exact sequence \[0 \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_V \longrightarrow \pi_*(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_{\bar{V}} \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0\] with \(\delta(C) < \delta(V)\), and we draw from this (by induction on \(\delta\)) that it suffices to take \(V\) normal.

Let \(B\) be the normalized of \(A\) in the body of rational functions on \(V\). Then \(B\) is a strictly local excellent ring of equal characteristics, and \(V\) is an open affine of \(\operatorname{Spec} B\), obtained by localizing with respect to \(x\). Let \(\hat{B}\) be the complement of \(B\), and \(\hat{V}\) the induced open (which is obtained from \(\operatorname{Spec} \hat{B}\) by locating with respect to the image of \(x\) in \(\hat{B}\)). According to 4.3, we have \[H^q(V, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) \simeq H^q(\hat{V}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell).\] We are therefore reduced to demonstrating that \[H^q(\hat{V}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > \delta(V) = \dim B = \dim \hat{B}.\] By induction hypothesis, the theorem is true if \(\delta(V) < d\), so theorem 6.1 bis applies in this situation. Consequently, it suffices to treat the case \(\dim \hat{B} = d\), and since \(\hat{B}\) is normal, we are in the situation of the lemma, hence the result.

6.7.0. We will first prove 6.6 under additional hypotheses, if \(B\) has characteristic \(p > 0\). Let \(A\) be an equidimensional, complete ring of equal characteristics and \(k \subset A\) be a body such that \(A/\operatorname{rad} A\) is a finite extension of \(k\). We say that the \(k\)-algebra \(A\) is formally separable if there exists a sub-\(k\)-algebra of \(A\) of the form \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_n]]\) such that the morphism \(\operatorname{Spec} A \to \operatorname{Spec} k[[x]]\) is finite and spreads to the generic point of \(\operatorname{Spec} k[[x]]\) (\(x=(x_1, \ldots, x_n)\)).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.7

Let \(k \subset B\) be a body above which \(B/\operatorname{rad} B\) is finite. Under the hypothesis of induction on \(d\) above, the conclusion of 6.6 is valid if the assumptions of 6.6 are satisfied and if in addition one or other of the following conditions is satisfied:

(i) \(k\) has characteristic \(0\).

(ii) There exists an element \(x \in \operatorname{rad} B\) which is invertible on \(U\) and such that \(\bar{B} = B/(x)\) is a formally separable \(k\)-algebra.

Demonstration. Let us choose elements \(x_1 = x, x_2, \ldots, x_d\) of \(\operatorname{rad} B\) such that \(\{x_1, \ldots, x_d\}\) generates a primary ideal for \(\operatorname{rad} B\). Then \(B\) is a finite \(A\)-algebra, where \(A = k[[x_1, \ldots, x_d]]\), and \(\bar{B}\) is the \(k[[x_2, \ldots, x_d]] = \bar{A}\)-algebra \(B/x_1 B\). As in case (ii) \(\bar{B}\) is analytically separable, one can in this case choose the elements \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\) in such a way that \(\bar{B}\) becomes a generically equate \(k[[x_2, \ldots, x_d]]\)-algebra. Then it immediately follows that \(B\) is also a generically \(A\)-algebra, which is also true, of course, in case (i).

Let \(b \in B\) be an element that generates the body extension \(R(B)\) on \(k((x))\). Let \(\varepsilon \in k[[x]]\) be the discriminant of the irreducible unitary equation of \(b\) above \(k[[x]]\). In case (ii), we can choose \(b\) so that \(x_1\) does not divide \(\varepsilon\). We further write \(\operatorname{Spec} B - U = V(x_1) \cup Y\), where \(Y\) does not contain any irreducible component of \(V(x_1)\). Let \(C\) be the ring \(\Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\), where we take \(Y\) with its reduced induced structure. By changing the \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\) if necessary, we can assume that \(V(x_d)\) is not contained in the image of \(Y\) in \(\operatorname{Spec} A\), and that moreover in case (ii) \(V(x_d)\) is not contained in \(V(\varepsilon)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.8

Let \(A_0 = k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{d-1}]]\{x_d\}\) be the strict localization of \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{d-1}]][x_d]\) at point \(x_1 = x_2 = \cdots = x_d = 0\). Under the above conditions, there exists a \(A^0\)-algebra \(B^0\), an open affine \(U^0 \subset \operatorname{Spec} B^0\), and an isomorphism \(A \otimes_{A^0} B^0 \simeq B\) such that the open of \(\operatorname{Spec} B\) induces from \(U^0\) i.e. \(U\).

The proof of this lemma is analogous to that of 1.5: it is enough to find \(A^0\)-algebras \(B^0\), \(C^0\) and isomorphisms \(A \otimes_{A^0} B^0 \simeq B\), \(A \otimes_{A^0} C^0 \simeq C\), because then there will exist ([3] 1.4) a morphism (surjective) and a single \(B^0 \to C^0\) which induces the morphism \(B \to C\). We therefore take \(U^0 = \operatorname{Spec} B^0 - \{(\operatorname{Spec} C^0) \cup V(x_1)\}\), and we immediately see that \(U\) is induced from \(U^0\). The existence of \(C^0\), and of \(B^0\) in case (ii), is proved as in 1.5. The existence of \(B^0\) in case (i) is a consequence of ([3] 5.1). (It can also be demonstrated directly by applying Abyankhar's lemma to branching along \(\{x_1 = 0\}\), and descent).

We can now complete the demonstration of 6.7. Applying 4.3 again, we find that \[H^q(U, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) \simeq H^q(U^0, \mathbb{Z}/\ell),\] from which it follows that it suffices to demonstrate that this last group is zero for \(q > d\). Now \(\operatorname{Spec} A^0\) is the limit of affine and splay schemes above \(k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{d-1}]][x_d]\), therefore \(\operatorname{Spec} B^0\) is the limit of finite type schemes above \(\operatorname{Spec} k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{d-1}]]\) of which each fiber is of dimension \(\leqslant 1\). Since \(U^0 \subset \operatorname{Spec} B^0[1/x_1]\), we have \[U^0 = \varprojlim U^0_\alpha\] where \(U^0_\alpha\) is affine of finite type above the scheme \(V^0 = \operatorname{Spec} k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{d-1}]][1/x_1]\) and where each fiber of \(U^0_\alpha/V^0\) is of dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

Let \(f^0 : U^0 \to V^0\) be the structural morphism. Since the fibers of \(f^0_\alpha\) are all of dimension \(\leqslant 1\), we have for \(v \in V^0\) \[\delta(\mathbb{Z}/\ell, f^0_\alpha, v) \leqslant \dim \mathcal{O}_{V^0,v} + 1 \leqslant d-1\] (because \(\dim \mathcal{O}_{V^0,v} \leqslant d-2\)). The induction hypothesis on \(d\) is therefore applicable to the morphism \(f^0\), and we find, by applying 6.1, \[R^q f^0_{\alpha*}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{au point } v \text{ si } q > \dim \mathcal{O}_{V^0,v} + 1,\] i.e. if \(q > d - \dim \bar{v}\). So \(R^q f^0_{\alpha*}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell) \neq 0\) at point \(v\) implies that \(\dim \bar{v} \leqslant d-q\). So for inclusion \[i : V^0 \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} k[[x_1, \ldots, x_{d-1}]] \quad (\text{cf } (6.0))\] we have \[\delta(R^q f^0_{\alpha*}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)) \leqslant d-q,\] and the recurrence hypothesis, in the form 6.1 bis, applied to the morphism \(i\), implies that \[H^p(V^0, R^q f^0_{\alpha*}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad p+q > d.\] Leray's spectral suite \[E_2^{pq} = H^p(V^0, R^q f^0_{\alpha*}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(U^0, \mathbb{Z}/\ell)\] therefore implies that \(H^n(U^0_\alpha, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) if \(n > d\), and as \(U^0\) is limit of \(U^0_\alpha\), we find that similarly \[H^n(U^0, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad n > d.\]
This completes the proof of 6.7, therefore of theorem 6.1 in characteristic 0.

footnotes.html: (∗) Depends on the resolution of singularities (see Introduction).
(∗) Depends on the resolution of singularities (see Introduction).

pages.title: 7. Affine morphisms - end of the proof.

blocks.html: 7. Affine morphisms - end of the proof.

Recall that to complete the demonstration of 6.1 in characteristic \(p > 0\) (still under the hypothesis of resolution), we were reduced by 6.6 to demonstrate the following assertion for \(d \geqslant 2\):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.1

Let \(B\) be a complete, normal local ring, of equal characteristics, with a separably closed residual body, and of dimension \(d\). Let \(U \subset \operatorname{Spec} B\) be an affine open such that it exists \(x \in \operatorname{rad} B\) which is invertible on \(U\). So \[H^q(U, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d.\]
Moreover, by induction hypothesis, we can assume that 6.1 is true for the values ​​of \(\delta < d\), and that 7.1 is already proven in the special case 6.7 (ii).

Let us first note that the condition that \(B\) is normal is not important. Indeed, let \(B\) be arbitrary, \(\pi : \bar{B} \to B\) the normalized of \(B\), and \(\bar{U} \subset \operatorname{Spec} \bar{B}\) the inverse image of \(U\). We have an exact sequence \[0 \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_U \longrightarrow \pi_*(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_{\bar{U}} \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0,\] where the support dimension of \(C\) is \(< d\), so \(H^q(U,C) = 0\) if \(q > d-1\). Since \(H^q(U, \pi_*) = H^q(\bar{U},.)\) (VIII 5.6), we see that \[(7.2)\qquad \begin{aligned} H^q(U, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) &= 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d \quad \text{équivaut à} \\ H^q(\bar{U}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) &= 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d. \end{aligned}\]

7.3.0. We will recall briefly and without demonstration the properties of the notion of formal separability that we need. The reader can consult (EGA IV 18.11.10, 18.11.11): Let \(A\) be a \(k\)-complete local Noetherian algebra whose residual field is a finite extension of \(k\), and let us note \(A'\) the complement of the local ring \(A \otimes_k k'\) (where \(k' = k^{1/p}\)). Then the extension \(A \to A'\) is root (possibly infinite), as we easily see — this is the well-known example of Nagata. It is therefore harmless for the calculation of flat cohomology to replace \(A\) by \(A'\) (VIII 1).

Now the condition on an equidimensional algebra \(A\) of dimension \(d\) to be formally separable, that is to say, to be a finite generically equal extension of \(k[[x_1,\ldots,x_d]]\), is expressed in terms of the module \(\hat{\Omega}^1_{A/k}\) (EGA IV 18.11.10), or the criterion Jacobian (EGA \(\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 22.6, IV 7.1) (∗), and we see that if \(A\) is not analytically separable, then the \(k'\)-algebra \(A'\) is not reduced. We can for example reduce ourselves for the demonstration to the case where \(A\) is given by an equation \(f = 0\), \(f \in k[[x_1,\ldots,x_{d+1}]]\) and apply the Jacobian criterion and the following lemma whose demonstration we leave pleasant to the reader:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3

Let \(f(x_1,\ldots,x_n) \in k[[x_1,\ldots,x_n]]\) be a formal series. Suppose that for each \(i = 1,\ldots,n\) there exists an invertible element \(u_i \in k[[x_1,\ldots,x_n]]\) such that \(u_i f\) is a series in \(x_i^p\) and in \(x_1,\ldots,x_{i-1},x_{i+1},\ldots,x_n\). Then there exists an invertible element \(v \in k[[x_1,\ldots,x_n]]\) such that \[vf \in k[[x_1^p, \ldots, x_n^p]].\]

It easily follows that by applying a finite number of times the process of replacing \((A,k)\) by \((\bar{A}',k')\), where \(\bar{A}'\) is the normalized of \(A'\), we return to the situation where \(A\) is indeed formally separable. Likewise, one can demonstrate that for a given \(x \in \operatorname{rad} A\), the ring \((A/(x))_{\text{réd}}\) will become a formally separable algebra by the same process, applied to the algebra \(A\).
By applying VIII 1.1 and 7.2, we find

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.4

It suffices to prove (7.1) under the additional condition that \(B\) contains a body \(k\) above which \(B/\operatorname{rad} B\) is finite, and such that the \(k\)-algebra \((B/(x))_{\text{réd}}\) is formally separable.
The proof of 7.1 in this case consists of a reduction to the particular case that we treated in 6.7 (ii):

With the ratings of 7.1, 7.4, i.e. \[D(x) = \sum_i r_i X_i\] the divisor of \(x\), so that each \(X_i\) is a closed irreducible subschema of \(\operatorname{Spec} B\) of codimension 1 which is formally separable above \(k\) (i.e., such that \(B/\mathfrak{J}(X_i)\) is a \(k\)-formally separable algebra, which is equivalent to the assertion that \((B/(x))_{\text{réd}}\) is formally separable). Let's choose \(y \in \operatorname{rad} B\) such that we have \[D(y) = \sum_i (r_i-1)X_i + \Gamma\] where \(\Gamma\) contains no \(X_i\), and consider bursting \[f : Z \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} B\] of the ideal \((x,y)\). The spectra of the rings \(B[y/x]\) and \(B[x/y]\) form an open affine covering of \(Z\).

Let \(B_1\) be the strict localization of \(B[x/y]\) in the maximal ideal generated by \((\operatorname{rad} B, x/y)\). For each closed point \(Q\) of the closed fiber of \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x] \to \operatorname{Spec} B\), let us note \(A_Q\) the strict localization of \(B[y/x]\) in \(Q\), and \(U_Q\) the open affine of \(\operatorname{Spec} A_Q\) inverse image of \(U\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.5

To prove 7.1 for the ring \(B\), it suffices to demonstrate that for \(q > d\) (\(\geqslant 2\)) we have

(i) \(\quad H^q(\operatorname{Spec} B_1[y/x], \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\)

and

(ii) \(\quad H^q(U_Q, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) for each \(Q\).

Demonstration. We have open immersions \[U \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} B[1/x] \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} B[y/x],\] so a diagram \[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.2em{ U \ar[rr]^-{i} \ar[dr]_{k} & & \operatorname{Spec} B[y/x] \ar[dl]^{j} \\ & Z & }\] If \(Q\) is a closed point of the closed fiber of \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x] \to \operatorname{Spec} B\), we have \((R^q i_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell)_Q = 0\) if \(q > 0\), by hypothesis. For each point \(P\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x]\) which is not closed in the closed fiber of \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x] \to \operatorname{Spec} B\), we have \(\delta(F,i,P) < d\), where \(F = (\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_U\) (notation of (6.0)). So by applying the recurrence hypothesis and 6.1 to the morphism \(i\), we find that for the morphism \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x] \to \operatorname{Spec} B\) we have \[\delta(R^q i_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell) \leqslant d-q.\] Consequently, the hypothesis of recurrence in the form 6.1 bis, applied to this last morphism, implies that \[H^p(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x], R^q i_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\] if \(q > 0\) and \(p > d-q\). With Leray's spectral suite \[E_2^{pq} = H^p(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x], R^q i_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(U, \mathbb{Z}/\ell),\] we reduce, for the demonstration of 7.1, to demonstrating that \[H^p(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x], i_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad p > d.\]

We have an exact sequence \[(7.6)\qquad 0 \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}/\ell \longrightarrow i_*(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_U \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0\] where \(\delta(C) < d\). It is therefore sufficient (again by induction) to demonstrate that \[(7.7)\qquad H^p(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x], \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad p > d.\]

But the open immersion \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x] \xrightarrow{\ j\ } Z\) is obtained by removing from \(Z\) the closed subset \(C\) "at infinity", and is identified in the other open \(\operatorname{Spec} B[x/y]\) from \(Z\) to the open \(C = V(x/y)\). The \(R^q j_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell\) are focused on \(C\), and the morphism \(C \to \operatorname{Spec} B\) is a closed immersion. Therefore, we have \(H^p(Z, R^q j_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) if \(p\) and \(q > 0\). Since the closed fiber of \(Z \to \operatorname{Spec} B\) is of dimension 1 and this morphism is proper, XII 5.3 bis implies that \(H^p(Z, j_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\) if \(p > 2\). The spectral suite \[E_2^{pq} = H^p(Z, R^q j_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x], \mathbb{Z}/\ell)\] therefore gives isomorphisms \[H^q(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x], \mathbb{Z}/\ell) \simeq H^0(Z, R^q j_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell) \simeq (R^q j_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell)_P\] if \(q \geqslant 2\), where \(P\) is the "infinity" point of \(Z\). These fibers are none other (VIII 5) than \[(R^q j_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell)_P \simeq H^q(\operatorname{Spec} B_1[y/x], j_* \mathbb{Z}/\ell).\]

According to 7.7, it is therefore sufficient to demonstrate that these last groups are zero if \(q > d\) (\(\geqslant 2\)). We have an exact sequence analogous to 7.6 which, combined with the recurrence hypothesis, shows that it is sufficient that \[H^q(\operatorname{Spec} B_1[y/x], \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d,\] hence the lemma.
Demonstration of 7.5 (i).

Consider the closed set \(C = V(x/y)\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} B[x/y]\), with the reduced induced structure. We immediately see that the closed immersion of \(C\) in \(\operatorname{Spec} B\) induced by the morphism \(Z \to \operatorname{Spec} B\) identifies \(C\) to the closed subschema \(X = \bigcup X_i\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} B\). Since \[\begin{aligned} D(x) &= \sum r_i X_i \\ D(y) &= \sum (r_i-1)X_i + \Gamma, \end{aligned}\] the element \(x/y\) generates the ideal \(\mathfrak{J}(X_i)\) locally at the generic point of \(X_i\), where the local ring is a ring of discrete valuation because \(B\) is normal. Consequently, the morphism \(\operatorname{Spec} B[x/y] \to \operatorname{Spec} B\) is an isomorphism in the neighborhood of such a point, and the element \(x/y\) vanishes with order 1 on each of the irreducible components \(C_i\) (corresponding to \(X_i\)) of \(C\).

Since the closed set \(C\) rises to \(\operatorname{Spec} B_1\), this remains true if we replace the ring \(B[x/y]\) by \(B_1\), so \(B_1/(x/y)\) is a complete ring which is a \(k\)-algebra formally separable.

To prove 7.5 (i), we can first apply 4.3, which allows replacing \(B_1\) with its complement \(\hat{B}_1\), which does not affect the ring \(B_1/(x/y)\). According to 7.2, we can also replace \(\hat{B}_1\) with its normalized \(\bar{B}_1\). Since \(B_1\) (hence \(\hat{B}_1\)) is already regular at the generic points of \(C_i\), the morphism \(\operatorname{Spec} \bar{B}_1 \to \operatorname{Spec} \hat{B}_1\) is an isomorphism at these points, so \(\bar{B}_1/(x/y)\) is still a formally separable \(k\)-algebra. We are thus brought back to the special case 6.7 (ii), hence the result.
Demonstration of 7.5 (ii).

Let's choose the point \(Q\) of the closed fiber of \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x] \to \operatorname{Spec} B\). The strict localized \(A_Q\) is written as limit \[A_Q = \varinjlim A\] where \(A\) iterates through a filtering category of finite-type slack rings above \(B[y/x]\). Consider the open affine \(U_A\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} A\), inverse image of \(U\). Since \(x\) is invertible on \(U_A\), the morphism \(U_A \to \operatorname{Spec} B\) is equal, and \(U_A\) is normal. Consequently, if we denote by \(C\) the normalized of \(B\) in the body \(R(A)\) of rational functions on \(A\), we have an open immersion \[U_A \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} C.\]

It suffices (VII 5.7) to demonstrate that for each \(A\), the image of \(H^q(U_A, \mathbb{Z}/\ell)\) in \(H^q(U_Q, \mathbb{Z}/\ell)\) is zero if \(q > d\). Since \(x\) is invertible on \(U_Q\), the morphism \(U_Q \to \operatorname{Spec} A\) factors with respect to \(\operatorname{Spec} A[1/f]\), if \(f \in B\) is an arbitrary element such that \(f\) is divisible by \(x\) in \(B[y/x]\) and that \(f/x\) does not cancel at the point \(Q\). It is thus sufficient to demonstrate that for such a suitable \(f\), we have \[H^q(U_{A,f}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d,\] where \(U_{A,f} = U_A - V(f)\) is the open affine of \(\operatorname{Spec} A[1/f]\), which is also an open affine of \(\operatorname{Spec} C\).

Let \(c \in k\) be an element such that \(y/x + c\) does not vanish in \(Q\), and let \(J\) be the ideal in \(B\) of the closed set \(\Gamma \cap X\). So if \[(7.8)\qquad f \equiv y + cx \quad (\bmod J^N) \qquad N > 0,\] the element \(f\) satisfies the above condition: Indeed, we immediately see that in \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x]\) we have \(V(J) = V(x)\). So (7.8) implies that \(f\) is divisible by \(x\), hence \[f/x \equiv y/x + c \quad (\bmod J).\] Since \(J\) vanishes at \(Q\), and \(y/x + c\) is not zero at \(Q\), this proves our assertion.

Now \(\Gamma \cap X\) is a closed subset of \(\operatorname{Spec} B\) of codimension 2. Consequently, we can choose a \(f\) satisfying (7.8), let's call it \(z\), such that \(V(z) \cap V(y)\) is also of codimension 2. Let us also choose elements \(x_3, \ldots, x_d\) such that \(y,z,x_3, \ldots, x_d\) is a system of parameters of the ring \(B\). Then \(B\), hence \(C\) too, is a finite \(k[[y,z,x_3, \ldots, x_d]]\)-algebra.

Let \(C^0\) be the normalized of \(k[[y,z,x_3, \ldots, x_d]]\) in the separable closure of \(k((y,z,x))\) in the body of rational functions \(R(C)\), so that \(C\) is a root extension of \(C^0\), and that \(C^0\) or a generically equable extension of \(k((y,z,x))\) (where \(x = x_3, \ldots, x_d\)).

Let's choose a linear combination \[f = az + by \qquad (a,b \in k),\] such as the morphism \[\operatorname{Spec} C^0 \longrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} k[[z,y,x]]\] is spread above the generic point of \(V(f)\), and such that \(f/x\) does not cancel at \(Q\) in \(\operatorname{Spec} B[y/x]\). It is enough to demonstrate that \[H^q(U_{A,f}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d,\] therefore (VIII 1.1) to demonstrate that \[H^q(U^0_{A,f}, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0 \quad \text{si} \quad q > d,\] where \(U^0_{A,f}\) is the open of \(\operatorname{Spec} C^0\) corresponding to the open \(U_{A,f}\) of \(\operatorname{Spec} C\), which is an affine open, as we see by descent (EGA IV 2.7.1 (xiii)). But by construction, the element \(f\) of \(C^0\) is invertible on \(U^0_{A,f}\), and \(C^0/(f)\) is a formally separable \(k\)-algebra. We are therefore in the situation of 6.7 (ii), which completes the demonstration of 7.5 (ii), therefore of 6.1.
